trigger: Rapid in-out movement on a recently opened low-activity account

triage.explanation: The account exhibits classic pass-through behavior: RM52,000 received from a logistics company and RM51,500 sent to a money changer within 2.5 hours, leaving a negligible balance. The account is newly opened (April 2026) and had minimal prior activity. There is no apparent commercial rationale for a personal account to receive and rapidly forward funds between these entities.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The rapid inbound from a logistics company and outbound to a money changer on a recently opened personal account lacks commercial rationale and shows irregular timing and diverse counterparties, consistent with pass-through laundering.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: Account holder Tan Wei Ming's newly opened personal account received RM52,000 from a logistics company, then within 2.5 hours sent RM51,500 to a money changer, leaving a negligible balance. There was minimal prior activity and no funds were retained for any stated purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound credit followed by outbound debit of a similar amount within a short window (2.5 hours)
Running balance spikes on inbound then drains back to near zero
Little to no funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated purpose of the account
Account is newly opened (April 2026) and had minimal prior activity
No apparent commercial rationale for a personal account to receive and rapidly forward funds between a logistics company and a money changer
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Same-day inbound and outbound flagged as rapid movement

triage.explanation: The inbound credits are legitimate card POS settlements typical for a kopitiam business, and the outbound transfer is a sweep to the same entity's operating account. The running balance does not drain to zero, the account is not newly opened or dormant, and the activity aligns with commercial rationale. This is consistent with a benign commercial sweep, not pass-through laundering.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The evidence shows a business receiving card settlement payments from two terminals and sweeping most funds to its own operating account, matching the benign look-alike of regular sweeps with commercial rationale.

transactions.counterpartyName: Kopitiam Aroma Sdn Bhd - Operating A/C

trigger: Account dormant ~16 months then sudden high-value throughput; no CDD refresh on file

triage.explanation: Account dormant ~16 months, no CDD refresh, large inbound followed by rapid near-total outbound, no retained funds, inconsistent with personal account history.

matchedTypology.name: Dormant-then-Active
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: No documented rationale or CDD update, and rapid onward movement of nearly all funds indicates suspicious reactivation.

typology.name: Dormant-then-Active
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: The account of Sarah Lee Mei Fong remained dormant for approximately 16 months before receiving a large deposit. Almost immediately after the funds arrived, nearly the entire balance was transferred out, leaving minimal residual funds.
groundsForSuspicion: Account dormant ~16 months then suddenly active with large throughput
No KYC/CDD refresh conducted upon reactivation
Large inbound funds rapidly followed by near-total outbound transfer
Activity profile inconsistent with account's historical personal use
No legitimate economic purpose apparent as funds were not retained
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Dormant account reactivated by a single large inbound credit

triage.explanation: Dormant account reactivated by a single large inbound credit from solicitors for property sale proceeds, followed by a stamp duty payment. The rationale is documented, funds are largely retained, and the activity is consistent with a legitimate property transaction. No indicators of suspicious rapid onward movement or lack of explanation.

matchedTypology.name: Dormant-then-Active
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10 (ongoing CDD); BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The evidence shows a documented property sale proceeds rationale with funds used for stamp duty, satisfying the benign look-alike.

transactions.counterpartyName: Messrs Tan & Co (Solicitors) - Property Sale Proceeds

trigger: Repeated cash deposits just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold across multiple branches and days

triage.explanation: Three cash deposits of RM24,200, RM23,800, and RM24,100 were made on consecutive days at different branches, all just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold. The aggregated amount of RM72,100 is substantial and was quickly moved out in a single transfer, consistent with structuring to avoid reporting and subsequent layering.

matchedTypology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

verifier.note: Cash deposits just below RM25,000 on consecutive days at different branches followed by a large outbound transfer strongly indicate structuring, with no documented commercial reason or consistent counterparties.

typology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

strDraft.activitySummary: Daniel Choo Kah Wai made three cash deposits over three consecutive days at different branches. Each deposit was just under the RM25,000 threshold, totaling RM72,100. The funds were then quickly transferred out in a single transaction.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple cash deposits structured just below the CTR threshold
Deposits made on consecutive days at different branches to avoid same-day aggregation
Total aggregated amount is substantial (RM72,100)
Funds quickly transferred out after accumulation, indicative of layering
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Routine daily cash takings just under the CTR threshold on an established retail account

triage.explanation: The cash deposits are consistent with a retail business depositing daily takings, which provides a documented commercial reason. The amounts, although just under the CTR threshold, appear to reflect genuine daily sales rather than deliberate structuring. The account maintains a running balance and makes supplier payments commensurate with a retail operation. No evidence of suspicious avoidance behavior.

matchedTypology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

verifier.note: The daily cash deposits are consistent with routine business takings for a retail account, not structuring, as there is no deliberate splitting across persons or days beyond normal operations.

trigger: Regular inbound credit followed by outbound debits flagged by velocity rule

triage.explanation: Alert shows a single payroll credit followed by routine bill payments over a day later; amounts are not similar, balance is retained, and no rapid pass-through pattern is present.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: The evidence shows a personal account receiving salary and making regular bill payments, which is consistent with benign look-alike activity, not the irregular timing and diverse counterparties characteristic of pass-through laundering.

trigger: Funds received and forwarded to a virtual-asset exchange within the hour on a new account

triage.explanation: New account (opened 2026-05-28) receives MYR 38,000 and within 42 minutes sends MYR 37,800 to a virtual asset exchange, retaining minimal balance. The rapid, irregular movement with no apparent commercial purpose matches the pass-through typology, likely layering through crypto.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Distinguishing test satisfied: irregular timing (single rapid transfer), diverse counterparties (individual sender to virtual asset exchange), and no commercial rationale for a personal account.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: The account, opened on 2026-05-28, received a single inbound transfer of MYR 38,000. Within 42 minutes, MYR 37,800 was sent to a virtual asset exchange, leaving a minimal balance. The rapid, near-total movement of funds with no other activity suggests the account is being used solely to facilitate a pass-through transaction.
groundsForSuspicion: Newly opened account (opened 2026-05-28) receives a significant credit.
Inbound credit of MYR 38,000 followed by outbound debit of MYR 37,800 within 42 minutes.
Outbound funds directed to a virtual asset exchange, a higher-risk destination.
Minimal residual balance (MYR 200) indicates no intent to use the account for normal banking.
No spending or activity consistent with the account’s stated purpose.
Pattern matches pass-through / rapid movement typology, often associated with layering.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Many small inbound payments from numerous parties flagged as fan-in

triage.explanation: The alert captures a typical day of a Lazada reseller receiving customer card payments for orders. The inbound payments are consistent with business activity, funds are retained in the account, and there is no outbound forwarding. This passes the distinguishing test for merchants receiving payments for goods/services, not mule consolidation.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: Evidence shows a business account receiving payments for orders with retained balance, consistent with a legitimate merchant, not mule consolidation.

trigger: Several inbound transfers from different individuals consolidated, then a payment forwarded onward; flagged by the fan-in velocity rule

triage.explanation: Alert matches fan-in/fan-out typology: multiple inbound transfers from unrelated individuals with no apparent relationship to the account holder, followed by a large outbound transfer to a company, inconsistent with a personal account profile. This pattern suggests consolidation and onward movement of funds, typical of mule activity.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: The account is personal, not business, and the outbound payment is partial, not rapid full forwarding, which aligns with the benign look-alike of a legitimate collection for goods/services.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: Aisyah binti Kamal's personal account received numerous small inbound credits from multiple unrelated individuals over a short period, with no apparent connection to the account holder. The funds were then aggregated and transferred onward in a single large outbound payment to a company, inconsistent with the account's typical activity and profile.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound credits from many unrelated individuals over a short period
Funds aggregated then forwarded onward in one large outbound transfer
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: International wire volume grossly inconsistent with the customer's declared student profile and zero declared income; no CDD update on file

account.holderName: Muhammad Faiz bin Razak (declared: full-time student, income RM0)

triage.explanation: The account, opened recently by a student with no income, received a large international wire (MYR 95,000) and within two hours transferred most of it (MYR 88,000) to an unconnected offshore entity, with the remaining balance quickly withdrawn via ATM. This matches pass-through laundering: funds rapidly moved, little retained, no commercial rationale, and account profile inconsistent with activity.

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

verifier.note: Transactions show irregular timing, diverse counterparties, and no commercial rationale for a student with zero income, satisfying pass-through laundering typology.

subject.holderName: Muhammad Faiz bin Razak (declared: full-time student, income RM0)

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: The account, opened recently by a university student with no declared income, received an international wire transfer of MYR 95,000. Within two hours, MYR 88,000 was transferred out to an unrelated offshore entity, and the remaining balance was quickly withdrawn via ATM, leaving the account near zero.
groundsForSuspicion: Recently opened account with no prior history
Account holder profile (student, zero income) inconsistent with high-value international transaction
Inbound wire of MYR 95,000 immediately followed by outbound transfer of MYR 88,000 within 2 hours
Rapid movement of funds with minimal retention, typical of pass-through laundering
Outbound transfer to an offshore entity with no apparent connection to the account holder
Remaining funds rapidly depleted via ATM withdrawal, suggesting layering
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Numerous inbound transfers from unrelated individuals rapidly consolidated and forwarded in full to a single offshore party

triage.explanation: Alert shows classic fan-in/consolidation pattern: five inbound transfers from unrelated individuals within three hours, aggregated to RM24,500, then immediately forwarded as a single RM24,000 outbound transfer to an offshore entity, leaving minimal balance. The account is a newly opened personal account, consistent with a mule account receiving and forwarding scam proceeds.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

verifier.note: Evidence shows personal account receiving rapid inbound transfers from unrelated individuals and forwarding nearly all funds offshore, matching mule consolidation pattern.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: Kevin Ng Jun Hao's newly opened personal account received five inbound transfers from seemingly unrelated individuals totaling RM24,500 within a three-hour period. Funds were immediately consolidated and a single outbound transfer of RM24,000 was sent to an offshore entity, leaving a minimal balance.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound credits from unrelated individuals in a short timeframe (five transfers in three hours)
Funds were aggregated and then forwarded in one large outbound transfer, consistent with layering
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Transaction pattern and volume are inconsistent with a personal account profile, especially for a newly opened account
Immediate transfer of nearly the entire balance to an offshore entity suggests funds are being moved to another jurisdiction quickly
Behavior is indicative of a mule account used to receive and onward transmit suspected scam proceeds
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).
